front: War: American invasions of Canada were repelled by Tecumseh and British/Canadian forces
War: partly caused by the British Orders in Council restricting neutral trade
War: a raid on Prophetstown (the Battle of Tippecanoe) pushed Tecumseh's confederacy to ally with the British before this war
War: the Chesapeake-Leopard Affair, involving the impressment of American sailors, was a direct cause
War: Jefferson's 1807 Embargo Act, limiting trade with France and Britain, preceded this war
Battle: forces under Roger Hale Sheaffe defeated Stephen Van Rensselaer's army on the Niagara frontier
Battle: Winfield Scott and William Wadsworth fought on the American side of this battle
Battle: British general Isaac Brock was killed here
War: the modern Citadelle of Quebec was constructed following this war
Battle: an October 1812 British victory that thwarted an American attempt to establish a base on the Canadian side of the Niagara River
Battle: Laura Secord walked twenty miles to warn James FitzGibbon of an impending American attack
Figure: rushed to help her brother James at the Battle of Queenston Heights before her famous 20-mile walk
Battle: an American attack here failed because the British had been warned by Laura Secord
War: Isaac Brock captured Fort Detroit with help from Tecumseh
War: William Hull's failed invasion of Canada preceded the capture of Fort Detroit
Figure: his surrender of Fort Detroit led to his court-martial
Figure: claimed to have personally killed Tecumseh at the Battle of the Thames
War: Thomas Van Horne's forces were ambushed at Sindathon's Village
War: general Adam Muir solidified his rout at the Battle of Brownstown in the ensuing Battle of the Oakwoods
War: Duncan McArthur's raid at the Battle of Malcolm's Mills ended a theatre of this war
War: the siege of Prairie du Chien took place in the same theatre of this war as the Battle of Malcolm's Mills
War: the Potawatomi won the Battle of Fort Dearborn during this war
War: the British took the trading post of Fort Mackinac
Battle: Oliver Hazard Perry's victory was announced with the note "we have met the enemy and they are ours"

front: Event: led by British general Robert Ross, occurring shortly after his forces won the Battle of Bladensburg
Event: the American retreat at the Battle of Bladensburg was nicknamed the "Bladensburg races"
Figure: ordered the Lansdowne portrait of George Washington rescued before fleeing the White House
Event: ended by a freak tornado
Figure: wrote a memoir recalling the burning of Washington, D.C.
Event: refugees sought shelter in Brookeville for a day before moving to the Octagon House
Event: a soldier paraded a captured hat around on a bayonet
Event: a man reportedly jumped in front of a cannon shouting "Are you Goths or Vandals?" to save the Patent Office
Event: a woman opposed "French John's" plan to booby-trap a house with gunpowder
Figure: reportedly ordered the destruction of all the letter "C" type in a newspaper building so his name could no longer be defamed in print
Figure: tried to recruit enslaved people from the American side by promising them freedom
Figure: wrote "The Star-Spangled Banner" after witnessing the bombing/siege of Fort McHenry
Work: later set to the tune of "Anacreon in Heaven"
Figure: a fugitive slave who defended Fort McHenry alongside Francis Scott Key
Figure: won the Battle of New Orleans in January 1815
Figure: this pirate, organized on the island of Barataria with his brother Pierre, was instrumental in the American victory at New Orleans
Figure: Governor William Claiborne put out an order for this pirate's capture
Figure: Commodore Daniel Patterson captured many of this pirate's supporters and ships
Figure: this pirate was eventually pardoned for his role in the war
Figure: this British major-general died leading troops at the Battle of New Orleans alongside Admiral Alexander Cochrane
War: Black regiments fought on both sides of the Battle of New Orleans
Figure: a drummer with the 7th Army Regiment at New Orleans, assisted in battle by Major D'Aquin's Battalion of Free Men of Color
Work: set to the tune of "Unfortunate Miss Bailey," celebrates the "Hunters of Kentucky" ("half a horse" and "half an alligator") defending the Chalmette Plantation
Figure: a "Rumpsey Dumpsey" campaign slogan referenced this War of 1812 veteran
War: the Hartford Convention proposed requiring a two-thirds majority in Congress to admit new states or declare war
Figure: financed the war largely through deficit spending as Treasury Secretary
Figure: gave up commerce at the war's onset, becoming a farmer and writing pacifist letters
War: an American expedition struggled to cross the Black Swamp after a militia mutinied over clothing rations
Treaty: formally ended the War of 1812, though the Battle of New Orleans was fought before news of it arrived
Treaty: limited naval presence on Lake Champlain and the Great Lakes, signed after the War of 1812's conclusion
War: Black Refugees and Black Loyalists were sent to Nova Scotia after fighting for the British in this war
Treaty: negotiated by Potawatomi leaders Billy Caldwell and Alexander Robinson in the war's aftermath
War: the wartime performance of Charles de Salaberry's Voltigeurs was mythologized by the Chateau Clique, boosting its postwar prominence alongside the Family Compact
